notes: Grade the chunk-map method DECISIONS: size by reasoning-capacity (not a fixed line count), git co-change as the PRIMARY boundary signal (imports second — they miss hidden coupling), lazy staleness derived via git diff against a recorded sha (never a stored flag), and seam-leak detection by grepping cross-chunk refs to non-exposed symbols. The naive answer (no skill) tends to: split strictly at a line-count threshold; use only the import graph; store an is_stale flag; silently widen a seam. Inline Q&A; read-only harness.

evals.name: size-by-reasoning-not-linecount
evals.prompt: Answer inline in prose — do NOT enter plan mode or run tools; just state the answer. I'm chunking a codebase for review. One file is a 240-line dense state machine; another area is 760 lines of flat data-transfer structs. Using the chunk-map approach, state how you decide chunk size for each, and whether a strict 'split every 400 lines' rule is right.
assertions: Sizes by reasoning capacity / one coherent responsibility, NOT a fixed line count (200–800 is a guide, not a rule)
Would keep the dense 240-line state machine as one chunk (or even cap smaller) and may keep the 760-line flat structs as one chunk
Rejects a strict 'split every 400 lines' rule; never splits mid-responsibility to hit a number
Split criterion = you can no longer state what it does / exposes / depends on without re-reading

evals.name: cochange-primary-boundary-signal
evals.prompt: Answer inline in prose — do NOT plan or run tools, just answer. Two files in different directories have NO import between them but they change together in almost every commit (a serializer and a schema). The import graph says they're unrelated. Using chunk-map, state which boundary signal wins and how you'd record this.
assertions: Treats git temporal CO-CHANGE as the primary boundary signal, ahead of the import graph
Says the import graph misses exactly this kind of hidden coupling
Groups the two together (or records them as a coupling/seam) despite no import — e.g. a couples_with edge in the map
Mentions deriving co-change from git history (e.g. git log --name-only) rather than imports alone

evals.name: lazy-staleness-not-stored-flag
evals.prompt: Answer inline in prose — do NOT plan or run tools, just answer. The chunk map was built at commit X; the repo has moved on. Using chunk-map, state exactly how you tell which chunks are now stale, and whether you store an is_stale field on each chunk.
assertions: Does NOT store an is_stale flag; derives staleness lazily at read time
Diffs changed files against the recorded reviewed_sha (e.g. git diff --name-only <reviewed_sha> HEAD) and maps them back to chunks
Only the chunks whose files changed are stale and get re-derived (not the whole map)
Frames the map as a snapshot at reviewed_sha, not a live index

evals.name: seam-leak-surface-not-widen
evals.prompt: Answer inline in prose — do NOT plan or run tools, just answer. While mapping, you find another chunk references a symbol that the owning chunk does NOT list in its exposed seam. Using chunk-map, state what this means and what you do.
assertions: Identifies it as a seam leak — a reference reaching past the chunk's declared interface into its internals
Verifies via grep for cross-chunk references to symbols not in the chunk's 'exposes' list
SURFACES it (either fix the coupling, or add the symbol to exposes deliberately) rather than silently widening the seam
Treats the declared seam as a contract that the leak contradicts
